system_prompt:
You are deriving structured "systems" from the distilled summary of a guitar method book.

A SYSTEM is a graph-with-rules. It has:
  - members[]: the taxonomy (the things this system operates over — chord
    types, position regions, voicing categories, scale forms, etc.)
  - traversal_rules[]: how you MOVE between members (preference for
    common-tone voice motion, pivot-finger shifts, position continuity,
    etc.)
  - modification_rules[]: how you ALTER a member (substitution lattices,
    voicing density changes, omission policies, color-tone requirements,
    etc.)

Each rule carries an `engine_payload` with a `kind` field. The kind is
either ONE of these 12 names (use exactly as spelled):

  PositionContinuity, VoiceMotion, StringSetTransition, SymmetryMovement,
  FamilyCoherence, SubstitutionExpand, DensityFloor, DensityCeiling,
  OmissionAllow, ColorToneRequire, NCTHarmonization, TextureCycle

OR a `_pending:<short-kebab-slug>` placeholder when no name in the list
clearly fits. When in doubt, USE `_pending:`. Do not stretch a name to fit.
The formal definitions of these kinds are pending (#298); your job today
is to pick the best label or honestly admit uncertainty.

Output a JSON object with this shape:

  {
    "master_id": "<from config>",
    "work_id": "<from config>",
    "systems": [
      {
        "id": "<master_id>:<work_id>:<system-slug>",
        "name": "<full system name>",
        "summary": "<one-paragraph prose>",
        "members": [
          { "id": "<slug>", "name": "<full name>", "summary": "<optional>" }
        ],
        "traversal_rules": [
          {
            "id": "<slug>",
            "name": "<full name>",
            "summary": "<one sentence>",
            "engine_payload": { "kind": "<kind-name or _pending:slug>" }
          }
        ],
        "modification_rules": [...]
      }
    ]
  }

Guidance:
- Three-segment system ids. Slug is kebab-case. Example:
  "benson:method-vol-1-chord-construction:harmonized-scale"
- Aim for 1-4 systems per book. A book usually has ONE central system plus
  optionally a few smaller ones.
- A system MUST have non-empty members AND at least one rule (traversal
  or modification) — empty systems are not useful.
- members[], traversal_rules[], modification_rules[] are all optional fields
  per the schema, but a real system has at least members + one rule kind.
- If the book teaches a system you can't structure as members+rules,
  describe it in `summary` and leave members/rules empty rather than
  inventing.

EVERY rule (traversal_rule, modification_rule, preference) MUST carry a
`references[]` array with at least one entry pointing back at the chapter
quote that backs the rule. Reference shape:

  { "chapter_n": <int>, "topic": "<topic label from chapter file>",
    "quote_excerpt": "<5-15 word snippet of the source quote>",
    "page": <int, optional> }

If a rule has no source quote it does not belong in the systems-draft —
write it into the system's `summary` field or omit it. References make
every kind assignment auditable; without them, this draft cannot be
reviewed against the source.

# Book-level distillation — jazz-guitar-method-vol-2-intermediate

_Aggregated from per-chapter summaries under `summaries/`._

## Overall Arc and Pedagogical Structure

This book is a structured jazz guitar method organized around a single load-bearing harmonic unit—the ii-V7-I progression—that is introduced in Chapter 1 and never fully leaves the stage. The method moves from harmonic foundation (Chapters 1–2) through comping and rhythm technique (Chapter 3), then through chord-specific lick vocabulary (Chapter 4), blues (Chapter 5), rhythm changes (Chapter 6), and turnarounds (Chapters 7–8), before closing with a capstone chapter (Chapter 9) on tone, practice strategy, and ear training. The arc is deliberately cumulative: each chapter either introduces a new harmonic context (blues, rhythm changes, turnarounds) or a new layer of vocabulary (licks, voicing substitutions, altered extensions) to be applied over frameworks already in place. Chapter 6 explicitly practices deferred complexity, asking students to master the sound of rhythm changes with familiar scales before tackling the advanced alterations that arrive later—a pattern visible throughout the method.

## Characteristic Systems and Terminology

The book's foundational unit of harmonic analysis is the **ii-V7-I progression**, established in Chapter 1 as the structure that establishes key and governs most improvisation decisions. Chapters 2, 3, 4, 7, and 8 all orbit around it. The master's naming system for fingerings—identifying positions by the string and finger holding the lowest root (e.g., "6/2" for sixth string, second finger)—is introduced in Chapter 1 and used throughout. Chapter 2 introduces the critical distinction between **essential chord tones** (3rd and 7th, which must be present) and **expendable tones** (roots and 5ths, which may be omitted), establishing **rootless voicings** as a practical consequence. Chapter 2 also introduces **swing eighths** as the rhythmic foundation of jazz phrasing, and **target-note technique** (also called *spelling out the changes*)—the practice of landing improvised lines on chord tones to articulate the underlying harmony. Chapter 3 extends this with three harmonic vocabulary devices for the V7 chord: voicing upper extensions via a Major 7th arpeggio rooted on the flat-7, substituting diminished triads from the 3rd, 5th, or 7th, and ascending scalar lines from chord tones. Chapter 3 also introduces the **three-string-set framework** for organizing voicings across the fingerboard.

## Rules and Prescriptions

Several explicit rules govern the method. Chapter 1 states the primary improvisation rule: **use the major scale of the I chord over diatonic ii-V7-I progressions**, with the hard exception that this rule is suspended if any chord contains altered tones (raised or lowered 5ths, 9ths, or 11ths) outside that major scale. Chapter 1 also defines arpeggios—via George Van Eps's phrase—as "melted chords," single notes of a chord sounded sequentially. Chapter 2 establishes that chord substitution must be judged by **context and ear, not theory alone**, and notes the practical symmetry of rootless 7b9 voicings, which can move at minor-third intervals without changing chord quality. Chapter 3 establishes the rhythmic rules for comping: **down strokes on downbeats, up strokes on upbeats**, with string damping to control sustain, and the aesthetic principle that swing rhythm is *implied* rather than mechanically predictable. Chapter 5 introduces three scalar tools for blues soloing—the **minor pentatonic scale**, **blues scale**, and **Mixolydian mode**—and Chapter 7 states the scaling rule for turnarounds: **four-measure turnarounds double the chord durations of their two-measure equivalents**.

## Methods and Lick-Based Vocabulary Building

The book's explicit pedagogical method is a cycle of **learn, transpose, apply**. Chapter 4 makes this most direct: learn sample licks for each chord type (major, minor, dominant, half-diminished), transpose to all twelve keys, and immediately insert licks into actual solos—never storing them in isolated study. Chapters 5 and 8 reinforce this by directing students to memorize licks from provided solo examples to build personal improvisational vocabulary. Chapter 3 introduces the foundational philosophy underpinning all of this: **improvisation is creative reorganization of learned vocabulary**, with licks functioning as word-like building blocks (analogous to scales as alphabet). Chapter 7 extends the method to turnarounds, directing players to memorize and transpose turnaround examples while actively listening to how other musicians deploy them, making **ear-training and transposition** the twin engines of mastery.

## Ear-First Principle and Advanced Practice

Chapter 9 crystallizes the method's deepest prescription: **hear first**. Tone is controlled by hands and technique, not equipment. Practice rules include focusing deeply on a small number of difficult areas rather than spreading effort thinly, rotating practice focus every few weeks, and prioritizing repertoire for live contexts. The chapter prescribes specific exercises to escape positional thinking: practicing scales, modes, and arpeggios on **single strings**, and improvising over recorded progressions on one string to force fresh ideas untethered from learned patterns. The culminating prescription for advanced students is to study one artist **deeply and comprehensively**—biography, compositions, transcribed solos, repeated listening—before moving on, making depth of immersion the mark of mature development. The single-string method and the hear-first principle together form the book's answer to the gap between knowing patterns and actually improvising: the brain must initiate the musical idea before the fingers execute it.

# Chapter 1 — Chapter 1

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch01.md`._

This chapter introduces the ii-V7-I progression as the foundational harmonic concept in jazz, explaining that it's essential because it establishes the key of the I chord. The chapter teaches that any ii-V7-I can have altered or extended chords while retaining its identity, provided the roots maintain perfect fourth spacing and the chord qualities remain minor, dominant, and major respectively. The primary improvisation rule is to use the major scale of the I chord when playing over diatonic ii-V7-I progressions, with the critical exception that this rule doesn't apply if any chord contains altered tones (raised or lowered 5ths, 9ths, or 11ths) that fall outside the major scale. The chapter also introduces arpeggios—defined through George Van Eps's memorable characterization as "melted chords" or single notes of a chord sounded sequentially—and establishes the scale and arpeggio fingering naming system used throughout the series, where fingerings are identified by the position of the lowest root (e.g., 6/2 for sixth string, second finger).

# Chapter 2 — Chapter 2

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch02.md`._

Chapter 2 teaches the harmonic and melodic foundations of jazz guitar improvisation, covering essential chord voicing rules, dominant chord variants (7b5, 7#5, 7b9), and the critical target-note technique for connecting improvised lines to underlying chords. The master's characteristic moves are visible in the emphasis on voice-leading priority—treating the 3rd and 7th as essential (and omittable notes as expendable)—and in the practical symmetry of rootless 7b9 voicings, which allow fingering patterns to move at minor-third intervals without changing chord quality. Load-bearing terminology introduced includes spelling out the changes (or targeting chord tones), neighbor tones (half- and whole-step approach devices), swing eighths (the rhythmic foundation of jazz phrasing), and slash-chord notation with its dual meanings (bass note vs. added extensions). The chapter also establishes the ii-V7-I progression as the core unit of jazz vocabulary and the principle that chord substitution must be judged by context and ear, not theory alone.

# Chapter 3 — Chapter 3

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch03.md`._

Chapter 3 teaches swing jazz comping—the accompanying technique guitarists use behind soloists—through both rhythmic and harmonic frameworks. Rhythmically, it covers strum direction (down on downbeats, up on upbeats), string damping to control sustain, and the aesthetic principle that swing rhythm is implied rather than predictable, leaving the guitarist to accent various parts with improvisation and surprise. Harmonically, it introduces three key vocabulary devices for the ii-V7-I progression: (1) playing a Major 7th arpeggio rooted on the flat-7 degree of the V7 chord to voice its upper extensions; (2) substituting diminished triads from the 3rd, 5th, or 7th of the V7 to create altered extensions; and (3) ascending scalar lines starting from chord tones, optionally alternated with descending patterns for contour interest. The chapter also introduces a three-string-set framework for organizing chord voicings across the fingerboard and explains rootless voicings—omitting roots to allow more extensions and rely on the bass player or ear-implied root. Finally, it establishes the foundational philosophy that improvisation is creative reorganization of learned vocabulary, introducing licks (pre-planned phrases for specific chords) as the word-like building blocks of solo vocabulary, analogous to scales as the alphabet.

# Chapter 4 — Chapter 4

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch04.md`._

Chapter 4 teaches jazz improvisation licks organized by chord type—major, minor, dominant, and half-diminished (min7b5)—along with the foundational ii-V7-I chord progression framework that appears throughout the chapter. The core pedagogical move is consistent: learn sample licks for each chord type, transpose them to all twelve keys, and immediately integrate them into solos, with progression difficulty escalating from three-beat chord-per-beat patterns to compressed one-measure ii-V7-I challenges. The chapter introduces the ii-V7-I pattern as a load-bearing structure and extends it across string sets to expand fretboard range, while establishing the principle that licks written for specific voicings (e.g., major 7th, dominant 9th) transfer to related extensions through careful ear training. The master's characteristic move is the emphasis on transposition discipline and immediate application—students must transpose every progression to all twelve keys and insert licks into their own solos as soon as they're learned, building fluency through active use rather than isolated study.

# Chapter 5 — Chapter 5

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch05.md`._

Chapter 5 introduces the blues as a foundational twelve-measure chord progression built on I-IV-V7 harmony, ranging from simple to complex depending on harmonic substitutions and passing chords typical of jazz blues. The chapter establishes three primary scalar tools for blues soloing—the minor pentatonic scale, blues scale, and Mixolydian mode—presented in C and intended for transposition across the fingerboard. A defining pedagogical move frames the gap between learning scale patterns and applying them: while beginners organize scales into fingerboard positions, mature improvisation combines these tools fluidly across the entire neck to match the ideas heard internally rather than confined patterns. The chapter's practice directive emphasizes memorizing licks from solo examples to build personal improvisational vocabulary, grounding conceptual knowledge in embodied musical material.

# Chapter 6 — Chapter 6

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch06.md`._

Rhythm changes, a chord progression originating from George Gershwin's "I've Got Rhythm," form a cornerstone of jazz vocabulary and have underpinned canonical works like Oleo, Anthropology, and The Flintstones. This chapter teaches soloing over rhythm changes using familiar scales from earlier in the method (establishing a foundation before moving to the highly altered and extended variations found in professional jazz). The master's characteristic pedagogical move is deferred complexity: the author asks students to become familiar with the sound of this progression before tackling the advanced alterations and extended scales that arrive later. Key terminology introduced includes rhythm changes itself and the concept of scale-based improvisation over this standard progression. Practice is anchored to a play-along method—recording or using the book's CD to practice soloing in real time—reinforcing the internalization of the progression's sound.

# Chapter 7 — Chapter 7

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch07.md`._

This chapter introduces turnarounds—two- or four-measure harmonic sections used at first endings or tune conclusions that smooth the transition back to the opening measure—and establishes them as a foundational vocabulary in jazz guitar performance. The chapter demonstrates the guitarist's leadership role in selecting turnarounds and emphasizes the performance rule that other players react to the guitarist's choice, making variety in turnaround vocabulary essential for creating unexpected arrangements and effective intros and endings. A key scaling rule states that four-measure turnarounds simply double the duration of the chords used in their two-measure equivalents. The load-bearing terminology introduced includes "turnaround" itself and the concept of "first ending," and the chapter directs players to memorize and transpose turnaround examples while listening actively to how other musicians deploy them—making ear-training and transposition practice the core skills for mastering this idiom.

# Chapter 8 — Chapter 8

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch08.md`._

This chapter teaches how to improvise through turnarounds by learning and memorizing transposable licks that create melodically smooth transitions back to tune beginnings, and it presents two synthesis pieces—a chord solo and a single-note solo, both loosely based on "Misty"—that reinforce the book's A-lesson chord-melody concepts and B-lesson improvisation techniques respectively. The load-bearing terminology introduced includes "turnarounds" as harmonic passages requiring specific improvisation strategies, and "licks" as reusable melodic phrases to be memorized and transposed across different chord progressions.

# Chapter 9 — Coda—A Medley of Suggestions and Musical Concepts

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch09.md`._

Chapter 9 is a collection of practical guidance on tone, practice strategy, and ear training that prioritizes foundational skill over external factors. The chapter teaches that hands and technique control tone more than equipment or environment, and establishes core practice rules: focus deeply on a few difficult areas rather than spreading effort thinly, prioritize repertoire for live performance contexts, and rotate practice focus every couple of weeks. Key exercises include practicing scales, modes, and arpeggios on single strings to escape positional thinking, improvising over recorded progressions on one string to generate fresh ideas, and singing along with your playing to train the ear to initiate musical ideas before execution. The master's characteristic moves visible here are the emphasis on hearing first as foundational to improvisation and the prescription for advanced students to study one artist deeply and comprehensively—reading biography, learning compositions, transcribing solos, and listening repeatedly before moving to the next. Load-bearing terminology introduced includes the hear-first principle and the single-string practice method as tools to bridge the gap between brain and fingers.

# Chapter 1 — Chapter 1

_Pages 6-16._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## ii-V7-I defined

> In jazz, the ii-V7-I is the most important chord
> progression. The ii-V7-I progression is so important, and so common, because its sound
> establishes the key of the I chord.

**Significance (p. 6).** Foundational definition of the chapter's central harmonic concept.

## ii-V7-I qualification rule

> Any and all chords in a ii-V7-I progression can be altered or extended. It will still be
> considered a ii-V7-I. For instance, Dmin9-G1 |-CMaj!3 would still be considered a ii-V7-
> lin C. As long as the roots are a perfect 4th apart and the qualities appear as minor,
> dominant and major respectively, a ii-V7-I exists.

**Significance (p. 7).** Defines minimum conditions for identifying a ii-V7-I.

## Improvising over diatonic ii-V7-I

> The rule for improvising over diatonic ii-V7-I progressions is quite simple, at least at this
> stage of the game: use the major scale of the I chord. If the progression is Gmin7-C7-
> FMaj7, improvise using the F Major scale; for Cmin7-F7-BbMaj7, use the Bb major scale, etc.

**Significance (p. 8).** The chapter's primary improvisation rule.

## Altered chord exception

> Be sure the chords in the ii-V7-I are not altered. In other words, all chords with a raised
> or lowered 5th (5, 5), 9th (9, 9) or 11th (11, 11) would make this rule inapplicable,
> because these altered tones are not diatonic (not in the major scale).

**Significance (p. 8).** Defines boundary condition for the major-scale rule.

## Arpeggios defined

> George Van Eps once said “arpeggios are melted chords and chords are frozen arpeggios.”
> That is pretty accurate. Arpeggios are simply the notes of a chord sounded one at a time

**Significance (p. 12).** Foundational arpeggio definition via memorable analogy.

## Scale fingering naming system

> The scale fingerings are named for the position of lowest root in the fingering. For
> instance, 6/2 means that the lowest root in the fingering is on the sixth string, and played
> with the second finger. Read the scale and arpeggio fingerings from left to right, starting
> on the lowest string and moving to the highest.

**Significance (p. 12).** Defines the position-naming convention used throughout the series.

# Chapter 2 — Chapter 2

_Pages 17-45._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Altered notes rule

> the only notes that can be altered in any chord are the Sth, 9th and the I Ith.

**Significance (p. 17).** Defines boundary of chord alteration in jazz harmony.

## 3rd and 7th as essential chord tones

> Remember that the two most essential notes in any chord are the 3rd and the 7th because
> they distinguish major, minor and dominant chords from each other. It is common to omit
> roots, 5ths, 9ths and | Iths in certain situations.

**Significance (p. 17).** Defines voice-leading priority: 3rds and 7ths are essential, roots/5ths/9ths/11ths can be omitted.

## Dominant 7b5 chord formula

> The dominant 7’5 chord is an extremely important sound in jazz. It is constructed from
> the following formula: Root-3-’5-'7. In the key of C this is C-E-G!-B’. In F it’s F-A-C’-E. In
> other words, it’s the old familiar V7 chord, but with a°5.

**Significance (p. 18).** Defines the 7b5 chord formula with concrete examples.

## Dominant 7#5 chord formula

> The dominant 745 chord is another very common sound in jazz. It is immediately recog-
> nizable. You will see it written a variety of other ways, such as 7+, 7aug and 7°13. The
> addition of the 9th is also common, in which case 9*5 or 9+ are the usual symbols. The
> formula for this chord is Root-3-45-'7. In the key of G, this would be G-B-D#-F. In the key
> of D it’s D-F-A#-C.

**Significance (p. 21).** Defines the 7#5 chord with formula and enharmonic variants.

## Spelling out chord changes (target notes)

> Instead of randomly playing
> the major scale over a progression, try to begin each phrase you play with a note of the
> chord that you are improvising over. This is called spelling out the changes, targeting chord
> tones, or using target notes.

**Significance (p. 26).** Names and defines the book's central improvisational technique.

## 3rds and 7ths in lines

> You may start your phrases from roots, 3rds, 5ths or 7ths of the chord. You will still use
> the major scale to improvise, it’s just that the first note of each phrase will be a chord
> tone. Improvisors tend to use 3rds and 7ths the most, because these notes are what give
> a chord its distinctive quality, but feel free to utilize roots and 5ths as well.

**Significance (p. 26).** Refines target-note technique with preference for 3rds and 7ths.

## Swing eighths definition

> In jazz it is standard practice to play eighth notes with a swing feel. While the music may be
> written as standard eighth notes, they will feel and sound like a series of eighth note
> triplets with a tie between the first two notes. When played in this manner, they are
> referred to as jazz eighths or swing eighths. Eighth notes played in a traditional manner are
> called straight eighths.

**Significance (p. 26).** Defines the foundational rhythmic convention governing all melodic examples.

## Dominant 7b9 chord formula

> Adding a lowered 9th to any dominant chord (79) gives a chord progression a very distinc-
> tive sound. Check out the formula: Root-3-5-7-9. In the key of E this is E-G*-B-D-F. In
> A it’s A-C-E-G-B.

**Significance (p. 24).** Defines 7b9 chord with formula and examples.

## 7b9 rootless fingering symmetry

> One interesting aspect of a 79 chord is that, when you drop the root, you can move the
> same fingering around the fingerboard at intervals of a minor 3rd without changing the
> chord’s quality.

**Significance (p. 24).** Defines symmetrical rootless 7b9 voicing shortcut.

## Neighbor tones technique

> The next step is learning to use neighbor tones. These are
> notes that an improviser uses to approach targeted chord tones. By far, the most com-
> mon neighbor tones are the notes one half step below and above the chord tone. Neigh-
> bor tones a whole step below and above are common too.

**Significance (p. 31).** Introduces and defines the neighbor tone approach device.

## Slash chord notation

> You will often see chord symbols with a slash (/) in them. This has two meanings. For
> instance, “F/G” means that an F chord is to be played, but with the note G added in the
> bass. Another usage of the slash, however, refers to quality. C6/9 means that the 6th and
> 9th degrees of the scale have been added to a C Major triad. C7/6 shows a C7 chord that
> also contains a 6th.

**Significance (p. 42).** Defines slash chord notation with two distinct usages.

## ii-V7-I importance rule

> It’s often said that you are only as good a jazz player as the number of ii-V7-I progressions
> you know. There is some truth to this. Knowing lots of fingerings will allow you to play
> with greater taste and variety.

**Significance (p. 43).** Establishes ii-V7-I as the core unit of jazz guitar vocabulary.

## Chord substitution context rule

> Remember that when enhancing or substituting chords, context is everything. Just
> because a substitution is “theoretically correct” doesn’t mean it sounds good in every
> instance. Experimentation is the way we find out what works, and is a large part of the
> fun.

**Significance (p. 34).** Tempers theoretical substitution framework with musical judgment principle.

# Chapter 3 — Chapter 3

_Pages 46-60._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Comping definition

> Comping is the term used to describe accompanying, or
> complimenting with chords. This is what jazz guitarists do
> when other players are soloing. We provide a harmonic back-
> ground. When playing with a keyboard player, it is preferable
> to take turns comping behind the soloist. If both the guitar-
> ist and the keyboard player comp freely at the same time the
> tune will become like a harmonic can of worms. Always listen
> carefully to what is going on around you.

**Significance (p. 47).** Defines comping and the rule about not doubling with keyboard.

## Swing strumming direction / damping

> It's a good idea to strum down Fon the downbeats and to
> strum up \ on the upbeats. This will help you keep your place
> in the measure. Also, try to damp the strings so that the
> chords don’t sustain during the rests. Damping is accomplished
> by either releasing pressure with the left hand, thereby halt-
> ing the sound, or by stopping the vibrating strings with the
> palm or wrist of the right hand. Different methods work de-
> pending on the situation. Experiment.

**Significance (p. 47).** Technique rules for swing strum direction and damping methods.

## Swing comping rhythm approach

> Unlike rock, pop and country music, the rhythm in a swing feel is implied. The beat is
> often suggested by the guitarist. Predictable rhythm parts are considered to be trite, so
> the guitarist is left to accent various parts of each measure, usually with an element of
> surprise and with a spirit of improvisation.

**Significance (p. 47).** Core aesthetic of swing comping: improvised unpredictable accents.

## Major 7th extensions over V7

> Simply play the notes of a major 7th arpeggio
> rooted on the ‘7 degree of the V7 chord in any ii-V7-I progression.
>
> For example, if the V7 chord is G7, you can play an FMaj7 arpeggio because F is the ’7 of
> the G7. Notice that this technique works nicely because you are actually just playing the
> upper extensions of the G7 chord: A is the 9th, Cis the I Ith and Eis the 13th.

**Significance (p. 49).** Defines Maj7-arpeggio-from-b7 technique and explains why it works as upper extensions.

## Starting Maj7 arpeggio off root

> Notice that you don’t have to start the major 7th arpeggio on the root of the Maj7 chord.

**Significance (p. 49).** Frees Maj7 arpeggio technique from root-position entry.

## Diminished extensions over V7

> Play a diminished triad starting at the 3rd, 5th
> or 7th degree of the V7 chord in any ii-V7-I progression. For instance, if the V7 chord is
> G7, you can play a Bdim arpeggio, a Ddim arpeggio or an Fdim arpeggio. These tones will
> resolve nicely into the I chord. This technique produces extensions of the G7 chord,
> some of which are altered: A’ is the’9 and C’ is the’! | (enharmonically the same as the 10,
> which is an octave above the 3).

**Significance (p. 51).** Defines diminished-triad substitution over V7 with three valid starting tones.

## Ascending lines from chord tones

> Simply play ascending scale-wise patterns starting
> from chord tones. Experiment on your own. Most players find this a very easy technique
> to incorporate.

**Significance (p. 53).** Ascending-lines device must begin on chord tones.

## Alternating contour rule

> Alternating ascending and descending patterns will give your line a more interesting contour.

**Significance (p. 55).** Melodic-contour rule combining ascending and descending devices.

## String-set chord organization system

> We will divide the six strings of the guitar
> into three string sets (6-5-4-3, 5-4-3-2 and
> 4-3-2-1 with 6 as the low E string and | as
> the high E string), and find the different
> inversions of specific chords going up,
> down and across the fingerboard. For ex-
> amples, we will use Dmin9 (ii), G9 (V7),
> CMaj7 (I) and Cé (also I) as the material
> for ii-V7-I progressions.

**Significance (p. 56).** Defines three-string-set framework for chord voicings in Chord Connections.

## Rootless voicings rationale

> Note that the roots have been omitted from both the Dmin9 and G9 voicings. This is to
> allow the use of more interesting chords. It will also give you experience organizing
> chords that have no root. Playing rootless chords often Permits the use of more exten-
> sions in chord voicings. The various voices can actually “imply” the sound of the root. Try
> to “hear” the root in your head when playing these chords. If you play in a group setting,
> the bass player can take care of playing the roots. Sometimes a bigger sound can be
> obtained this way.

**Significance (p. 56).** Explains rootless voicings and the ear-training of implying the root.

## Licks definition and role

> licks are a very important part of your vocabulary. If scales can be compared to our
> alphabet, then licks are comparable to words or sentences. Licks are groups of notes,
> phrases or runs that an improviser can use in solos. These are pre-planned phrases that
> the improviser knows will work over a particular chord or chord progression.

**Significance (p. 59).** Defines licks with scale/alphabet analogy as chord-specific vocabulary.

## Improvisation as creative reorganization

> To a large extent, improvisation is
> really the “creative reorganization” of things you already know.

**Significance (p. 59).** Book's foundational philosophy framing lick-learning as vocabulary building.

_Pages 61-73._

If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Major chord licks applicability

> Here are eight sample licks that work well over major-type chords. Transpose them and
> Start using them immediately in songs you are learning. These licks are labeled with major
> 7th chords but will work equally well with major 6th, major 9th and major I3th chords.
> Experiment and let your ear be your guide.

**Significance (p. 60).** Defines scope and transposition rule for the major licks.

## ii-V7-I chord connection pattern

> In this lesson, the chords you were working with in Lesson IA have been organized into
> li-V7-I patterns. Each chord receives one beat. Be sure to transpose these progressions
> to all twelve keys. Have patience, and enjoy the process. It can take some time to get
> comfortable with these chord connections.

**Significance (p. 62).** Establishes ii-V7-I framework, one-beat-per-chord, transposition requirement.

## Minor chord licks instruction

> Here are some sample licks to use over minor chords. Once again, learn, transpose and
> insert these into your solos as soon as possible!

**Significance (p. 64).** Encapsulates learn-then-apply pedagogy for minor chord licks.

## Moving across string sets

> By now, these chords are surely getting easier for you to handle. The following ii-V7-I progressions move across the string sets. Once again, each chord receives one beat. Transpose these to all twelve keys.

**Significance (p. 67).** Introduces shifting ii-V7-I across string sets to expand fretboard range.

## Dominant chord licks scope

> Here are some licks that will work well over unaltered dominant chords. Try using them
> over dominant 9th, | Ith and 13th chords as well. Transpose them and apply them to
>
> your solos.

**Significance (p. 68).** Defines applicability of dominant licks over 9th, 11th, 13th voicings.

## One-measure ii-V7-I challenge

> Once the examples in Lesson 3A get easier, challenge yourself with these. They are arranged in one-measure ii-V7-I patterns. Take your time learning each example and then transpose them.

**Significance (p. 71).** Raises difficulty by compressing ii-V7-I into one measure.

## min7b5 chord licks

> There are many ways to improvise over min7’5 (half-diminished) chords. Here are just a
> few licks to help you get used to the sound.

**Significance (p. 72).** Introduces half-diminished chord by alternate name as ear-training.

# Chapter 5 — Chapter 5

_Pages 74-82._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Blues as jazz form definition

> For our purposes, the blues will be thought of as a form; it is a twelve measure chord
> progression that is based around a I-IV-V7 progression. As you will see, the blues progres-
> sion can be very simple or very complex.

**Significance (p. 74).** Defines structural foundation of blues form.

## Jazz blues substitutions rule

> Jazz
> blues progressions tend to use quite a few substitutions and passing chords.

**Significance (p. 74).** Core harmonic rule distinguishing jazz blues.

## Soloing tools: scale choices

> This
> lesson begins with a review of the minor pentatonic scale, blues scale and Mixolydian
> mode fingerings. They appear here in the key of C, but you should practice transposing
> them. Just move to any other root of your choice and work through the same fingerings.

**Significance (p. 78).** Establishes three primary scalar tools for blues soloing with transposition.

## Scales vs. fingerboard application

> There is a big difference between learning improvisational tools, such as scales, and actu-
> ally putting them to use. At first, we tend to organize them into patterns in different areas
> of the fingerboard. In actual practice, we usually combine the various tools and utilize the
> entire fingerboard. This is because the ideas we hear in our heads don’t always conform to
> organized fingering patterns.

**Significance (p. 79).** Central pedagogical rule: combine scale tools across whole neck.

## Memorizing solo examples

> Study these solos and
>
> memorize the parts that you enjoy so you can use them in your own solos.

**Significance (p. 79).** Practice directive: internalize licks for personal vocabulary.

_Pages 83-85._

If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Rhythm Changes definition

> Just as the blues form is part of our "stock and trade," rhythm changes are a very important part of our vocabulary. This chord progression was originally found in the song I've Got Rhythm by George Gershwin. It has been the basis for literally dozens of songs, and has been a favorite progression of improvisers for decades.

**Significance (p. 83).** Defines rhythm changes as foundational jazz concept with origin.

## Rhythm Changes example songs

> Oleo, by Sonny Rollins, Anthropology by Charlie Parker and The Flintstones would be examples of songs based on these changes. There are actually many variations on this progression, but the example below represents its most basic form.

**Significance (p. 83).** Anchors concept to canonical repertoire.

## Soloing over Rhythm Changes — scale usage

> This solo utilizes scales that you learned in both this book and Beginning Jazz Guitar. As you progress, you'll find that rhythm changes are usually highly altered and extended, so that more interesting scales and improvisational devices can be used. For now, just become familiar with the sound of this progression.

**Significance (p. 84).** Pedagogy: start with familiar scales, internalize the sound before alterations.

## Practice method — play-along

> Use the CD that is available with this book, or record yourself playing the changes, so that you can practice playing along and improvising your own solo.

**Significance (p. 84).** Prescribes play-along practice for developing rhythm-changes improvisation.

## Turnaround definition

> A turnaround is a two- or four-measure section at a first ending, or at the end of a tune. A turnaround creates a smooth harmonic transition back to the first measure.

**Significance (p. 86).** Foundational definition of turnaround.

## Turnaround usage contexts

> You will find turnarounds in most jazz tunes. Generally, the guitarist can choose to play any one of the many kinds of turnarounds possible and it's up to the other players to react. For variety's sake, it pays to know a lot of different turnarounds. Using a variety of turnarounds will create unexpected twists and turns in an arrangement. They also make good intros and endings.

**Significance (p. 86).** Performance rule: guitarist leads turnaround choices, value of variety.

## Four-measure turnaround rule

> These are examples of two-measure turnarounds going to C. When playing four-measure turnarounds, the duration of the chords would simply be doubled.

**Significance (p. 86).** Scaling rule from two to four-measure turnarounds.

## Practice instruction

> Memorize and transpose these and keep your ears open to those other players use.

**Significance (p. 86).** Practice directive: internalize examples and learn from other musicians.

## Soloing over turnarounds

> Obviously, if turnarounds are common, you will need to know how to improvise your way through them. Create licks that you know will work over various turnarounds. This will help you make melodically smoother transitions back to the beginnings of your tunes. Study the following turnarounds going to C. Memorize and transpose the ones you like.

**Significance (p. 88).** Core practice rule for turnaround improvisation: learn, memorize, transpose licks.

## Chord solo purpose & synthesis

> Here is a chord solo loosely based on the changes of the song Misty. This arrangement incorporates many of the concepts that have been covered in the "A" lessons of this book. Use it to reinforce your understanding of what you have learned.

**Significance (p. 89).** Frames chord solo as synthesis of book's A-lesson chord-melody concepts.

## Single-note solo purpose & synthesis

> Here is a single note solo also loosely based on the chord changes of Misty. This solo incorporates many of the concepts that have been covered in the "B" lessons of this book. Use it to solidify what you have learned.

**Significance (p. 91).** Frames single-note solo as synthesis of book's B-lesson improvisation concepts.

# Chapter 9 — Coda—A Medley of Suggestions and Musical Concepts

_Pages 92-98._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Tone: hands vs. gear

> In spite of all the external factors, your hands control the tone of your instrument more
> than any other factor. It pays to learn and maintain good technique. More than any room
> or effects processor, it will give you greater control of nuance. This is as true for electric
> guitarists as it is for acoustic players. Don’t rely on rooms and electronics for your sound.
> Count on your own technique to ensure a consistently good sound.

**Significance (p. 92).** Core tone rule: physical technique over equipment.

## Practice focus strategy

> It is generally best to focus on a few aspects of your playing,
> but work on them very hard.

**Significance (p. 92).** Foundational rule for practice: depth over breadth.

## Practicing difficult material

> Practicing things you find easy is a waste of time. Practice those things that are most
> difficult for you. Try to cover two or three of these areas daily, and maintain the same
> routine for just a couple of weeks. Then, when you are ready, change it.

**Significance (p. 93).** Rule for selecting practice content and rotation cycles.

## Practicing for the gig

> By far, practicing for the gig is the most important thing. This is where you are being heard.
> This gig, hopefully, will lead you to the next gig, and the next and so on.

**Significance (p. 93).** Hierarchy-topping rule: performance context drives practice priorities.

## Single-string practice method

> | suggest that you start learning all of your scales, modes
> and arpeggios up and down single strings. In fact, you could even practice reading music
> this way. After a while, try working on sets of two adjacent strings, then try it on sets of
> three strings.

**Significance (p. 93).** Progressive single-string exercise to break positional thinking.

## Single-string improvisation exercise

> The easiest way to discover this for
> yourself is to record a progression and improvise using only one string. At first, your lack
> of familiarity will be evident, but after a while you may find that your ideas are radically
> different than usual.

**Significance (p. 93).** Concrete exercise for generating fresh improvisational ideas.

## Hearing before playing

> When you improvise, the ideal is to first hear an idea in your head, and then execute it.

**Significance (p. 94).** Foundational principle of musical intention for improvisation.

## Singing to train inner ear

> Singing along with what you are playing is a good way to begin training yourself to hear
> first. Sing or hum along as you play, trying to duplicate all the pitches and rhythms.
> Eventually you will find that your singing initiates your solo ideas. This is how you will
> begin to bridge the gap between your brain and your fingers.

**Significance (p. 94).** Vocal doubling technique to develop hear-first skill.

## Deep study of one artist

> It is a good idea for more advanced students to study one artist exclusively for a while.
> During this time read as much about the artist's life as you can find, learn several of their
> compositions, transcribe a few of their solos, and listen to their recordings constantly.
> Then move on to another artist.

**Significance (p. 94).** Multi-faceted immersion: biography, composition, transcription, listening.
